MINI MINUTES FOR JUN-10-2014 09:30 AM FOR DEPT 302

LAW AND MOTION 302, RULING: THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING. DEFENDANT PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND BECAUSE THE SUPERIOR COURT LACKS JURISDICTION TO ENTERTAIN THE INSTANT SUIT PURSUANT TO CAL. PUBLIC UTILITIES CODE SEC. 1759, SAN DIEGO GAS & ELECTRIC CO. V. SUP. CRT. (1996) 13 CAL.4TH 893 ("COVALT") AND FORD ET AL V. PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC CO. (1997) 60 CAL.APP.4TH 696. SEE CCP 430.10(A). IN SAN DIEGO GAS & ELECTRIC CO. V. SUP. CRT. (1996) 13 CAL.4TH 893, 917-918 ("COVALT"), THE COURT PROVIDES THAT THE CPUC HAS EXCLUSIVE JURISDICTION WHERE "(1) THE COMMISSION HAD THE AUTHORITY TO ADOPT A REGULATORY POLICY; (2) THE COMMISSION HAS EXERCISED ITS AUTHORITY; AND (3) THE SUPERIOR ACTION WOULD HINDER OR INTERFERE WITH THE COMMISSION'S EXERCISE OF REGULATORY AUTHORITY." ID. 13 CAL.4TH 917-918. HERE, THE DEFENDANTS CORRECTLY NOTE THAT THE CPUC HAS THE AUTHORITY TO ADOPT SMARTMETER POLICY UNDER PUBLIC UTILITIES CODE SECS. 768, 451, 761, AND 762. THE CPUC EXERCISED ITS AUTHORITY, ALLOWED THE INSTALLATION OF THE SMART METERS, AND CONCLUDED THAT THEY WERE SAFE AND IN COMPLIANCE WITH FCC REGULATIONS. ALLOWING THIS ACTION TO PROCEED WOULD INTERFERE WITH THE CPUC'S REGULATORY AUTHORITY. PLAINTIFF ARGUES THAT THE ACTION IS PROPER UNDER CAL. PUBLIC UTILITIES SEC. 2106. AS FORD ET AL V. PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC CO. (1997) 60 CAL.APP.4TH 696 EXPLAINS, SEC. 2106 HAS BEEN LIMITED TO THOSE SITUATIONS IN WHICH AN AWARD OF DAMAGES WOULD NOT HINDER OR FRUSTRATE THE CPUC'S DECLARED REGULATORY POLICY. SEC. 1759 BARS AN ACTION UNDER SEC. 2106 NOT ONLY WHEN AN AWARD OF DAMAGES WOULD DIRECTLY CONTRAVENE A SPECIFIC DECISION OF THE CPUC, BUT ALSO WHEN AN AWARD WOULD SIMPLY HAVE THE EFFECT OF UNDERMINING A GENERAL REGULATORY POLICY. THE CPUC HAS DETERMINED THAT SMARTMETERS CAN BE INSTALLED AND POSE NO UNDUE SAFETY RISK. PLAINTIFF'S SUIT CLEARLY ATTEMPTS TO UNDERMINE THAT POLICY. ORDER SIGNED.

DEFENDANT LANDIS+GYR, INC'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND BECAUSE THE SUPERIOR COURT LACKS JURISDICTION TO ENTERTAIN THE INSTANT SUIT PURSUANT TO CAL. PUBLIC UTILITIES CODE SEC. 1759, SAN DIEGO GAS & ELECTRIC CO. V. SUP. CRT. (1996) 13 CAL.4TH 893 ("COVALT") AND FORD ET AL V. PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC CO. (1997) 60 CAL.APP.4TH 696. SEE CCP 430.10(A). IN SAN DIEGO GAS & ELECTRIC CO. V. SUP. CRT. (1996) 13 CAL.4TH 893, 917-918 ("COVALT"), THE COURT PROVIDES THAT THE CPUC HAS EXCLUSIVE JURISDICTION WHERE "(1) THE COMMISSION HAD THE AUTHORITY TO ADOPT A REGULATORY POLICY; (2) THE COMMISSION HAS EXERCISED ITS AUTHORITY; AND (3) THE SUPERIOR ACTION WOULD HINDER OR INTERFERE WITH THE COMMISSION'S EXERCISE OF REGULATORY AUTHORITY." ID. 13 CAL.4TH 917-918. HERE, THE DEFENDANTS CORRECTLY NOTE THAT THE CPUC HAS THE AUTHORITY TO ADOPT SMARTMETER POLICY UNDER PUBLIC UTILITIES CODE SECS. 768, 451, 761, AND 762. THE CPUC EXERCISED ITS AUTHORITY, ALLOWED THE INSTALLATION OF THE SMART METERS, AND CONCLUDED THAT THEY WERE SAFE AND IN COMPLIANCE WITH FCC REGULATIONS. ALLOWING THIS ACTION TO PROCEED WOULD INTERFERE WITH THE CPUC'S REGULATORY AUTHORITY. PLAINTIFF ARGUES THAT THE ACTION IS PROPER UNDER CAL. PUBLIC UTILITIES SEC. 2106. AS FORD ET AL V. PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC CO. (1997) 60 CAL.APP.4TH 696 EXPLAINS, SEC. 2106 HAS BEEN LIMITED TO THOSE SITUATIONS IN WHICH AN AWARD OF DAMAGES WOULD NOT HINDER OR FRUSTRATE THE CPUC'S DECLARED REGULATORY POLICY. SEC. 1759 BARS AN ACTION UNDER SEC. 2106 NOT ONLY WHEN AN AWARD OF DAMAGES WOULD DIRECTLY CONTRAVENE A SPECIFIC DECISION OF THE CPUC, BUT ALSO WHEN AN AWARD WOULD SIMPLY HAVE THE EFFECT OF UNDERMINING A GENERAL REGULATORY POLICY. THE CPUC HAS DETERMINED THAT SMARTMETERS CAN BE INSTALLED AND POSE NO UNDUE SAFETY RISK. PLAINTIFF'S SUIT CLEARLY ATTEMPTS TO UNDERMINE THAT POLICY. ORDER SIGNED.

LAW AND MOTION 302, RULING: THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING. DEFENDANT WELLINGTON POWER CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND BECAUSE THE SUPERIOR COURT LACKS JURISDICTION TO ENTERTAIN THE INSTANT SUIT PURSUANT TO CAL. PUBLIC UTILITIES CODE SEC. 1759, SAN DIEGO GAS & ELECTRIC CO. V. SUP. CRT. (1996) 13 CAL.4TH 893 ("COVALT") AND FORD ET AL V. PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC CO. (1997) 60 CAL.APP.4TH 696. SEE CCP 430.10(A). IN SAN DIEGO GAS & ELECTRIC CO. V. SUP. CRT. (1996) 13 CAL.4TH 893, 917-918 ("COVALT"), THE COURT PROVIDES THAT THE CPUC HAS EXCLUSIVE JURISDICTION WHERE "(1) THE COMMISSION HAD THE AUTHORITY TO ADOPT A REGULATORY POLICY; (2) THE COMMISSION HAS EXERCISED ITS AUTHORITY; AND (3) THE SUPERIOR ACTION WOULD HINDER OR INTERFERE WITH THE COMMISSION'S EXERCISE OF REGULATORY AUTHORITY." ID. 13 CAL.4TH 917-918. HERE, THE DEFENDANTS CORRECTLY NOTE THAT THE CPUC HAS THE AUTHORITY TO ADOPT SMARTMETER POLICY UNDER PUBLIC UTILITIES CODE SECS. 768, 451, 761, AND 762. THE CPUC EXERCISED ITS AUTHORITY, ALLOWED THE INSTALLATION OF THE SMART METERS, AND CONCLUDED THAT THEY WERE SAFE AND IN COMPLIANCE WITH FCC REGULATIONS. ALLOWING THIS ACTION TO PROCEED WOULD INTERFERE WITH THE CPUC'S REGULATORY AUTHORITY. PLAINTIFF ARGUES THAT THE ACTION IS PROPER UNDER CAL. PUBLIC UTILITIES SEC. 2106. AS FORD ET AL V. PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC CO. (1997) 60 CAL.APP.4TH 696 EXPLAINS, SEC. 2106 HAS BEEN LIMITED TO THOSE SITUATIONS IN WHICH AN AWARD OF DAMAGES WOULD NOT HINDER OR FRUSTRATE THE CPUC'S DECLARED REGULATORY POLICY. SEC. 1759 BARS AN ACTION UNDER SEC. 2106 NOT ONLY WHEN AN AWARD OF DAMAGES WOULD DIRECTLY CONTRAVENE A SPECIFIC DECISION OF THE CPUC, BUT ALSO WHEN AN AWARD WOULD SIMPLY HAVE THE EFFECT OF UNDERMINING A GENERAL REGULATORY POLICY. THE CPUC HAS DETERMINED THAT SMARTMETERS CAN BE INSTALLED AND POSE NO UNDUE SAFETY RISK. PLAINTIFF'S SUIT CLEARLY ATTEMPTS TO UNDERMINE THAT POLICY. ORDER SIGNED.

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-10-2014 Off Calendar

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-10-2014 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Donald Zimmer, Esq., 415.318.1220, for defendant Landis + Gyr, Inc.; No other appearances.

RULING: The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT LANDIS+GYR, INC.'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT is off calendar as a duplicate entry. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Not reported.

MOTION TO STRIKE 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-10-2014 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Donald Zimmer, Esq., 415.318.1220, for defendant Landis + Gyr, Inc.; No other appearances.

RULING: The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT LANDIS+GYR, INCORPORATED'S MOTION TO STRIKE 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT is off calendar as moot based on the demurrer ruling. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Not reported.

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-10-2014 Sustained
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Erin Poppler, Esq., 415.397.9006, for defendant Wellington Power Corporation; No other appearances.

RULING: The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT WELLINGTON POWER CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT is sustained without leave to amend because the Superior Court lacks jurisdiction to entertain the instant suit pursuant to Cal. Public Utilities Code sec. 1759, San Diego Gas & Electric Co. v. Sup. Crt. (1996) 13 Cal.4th 893 ("Covalt") and Ford et al v. Pacific Gas and Electric Co. (1997) 60 Cal.App.4th 696. See CCP 430.10(a). In San Diego Gas & Electric Co. v. Sup. Crt. (1996) 13 Cal.4th 893, 917-918 ("Covalt"), the court provides that the CPUC has exclusive jurisdiction where "(1) the commission had the authority to adopt a regulatory policy; (2) the commission has exercised its authority; and (3) the superior action would hinder or interfere with the commission's exercise of regulatory authority." Id. 13 Cal.4th 917-918. Here, the defendants correctly note that the CPUC has the authority to adopt SmartMeter policy under Public Utilities Code secs. 768, 451, 761, and 762. The CPUC exercised its authority, allowed the installation of the Smart Meters, and concluded that they were safe and in compliance with FCC regulations. Allowing this action to proceed would interfere with the CPUC's regulatory authority. Plaintiff argues that the action is proper under Cal. Public Utilities sec. 2106. As Ford et al v. Pacific Gas and Electric Co. (1997) 60 Cal.App.4th 696 explains, sec. 2106 has been limited to those situations in which an award of damages would not hinder or frustrate the CPUC's declared regulatory policy. Sec. 1759 bars an action under sec. 2106 not only when an award of damages would directly contravene a specific decision of the CPUC, but also when an award would simply have the effect of undermining a general regulatory policy. The CPUC has determined that SmartMeters can be installed and pose no undue safety risk. Plaintiff's suit clearly attempts to undermine that policy. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Reporter: Sherry Sawyer #5976.

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-10-2014 Sustained
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Daralyn Durie, Esq., 415.362.6666, for defendant Pacific Gas and Electric Company; No other appearances.

RULING: The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT is sustained without leave to amend because the Superior Court lacks jurisdiction to entertain the instant suit pursuant to Cal. Public Utilities Code sec. 1759, San Diego Gas & Electric Co. v. Sup. Crt. (1996) 13 Cal.4th 893 ("Covalt") and Ford et al v. Pacific Gas and Electric Co. (1997) 60 Cal.App.4th 696. See CCP 430.10(a). In San Diego Gas & Electric Co. v. Sup. Crt. (1996) 13 Cal.4th 893, 917-918 ("Covalt"), the court provides that the CPUC has exclusive jurisdiction where "(1) the commission had the authority to adopt a regulatory policy; (2) the commission has exercised its authority; and (3) the superior action would hinder or interfere with the commission's exercise of regulatory authority." Id. 13 Cal.4th 917-918. Here, the defendants correctly note that the CPUC has the authority to adopt SmartMeter policy under Public Utilities Code secs. 768, 451, 761, and 762. The CPUC exercised its authority, allowed the installation of the Smart Meters, and concluded that they were safe and in compliance with FCC regulations. Allowing this action to proceed would interfere with the CPUC's regulatory authority. Plaintiff argues that the action is proper under Cal. Public Utilities sec. 2106. As Ford et al v. Pacific Gas and Electric Co. (1997) 60 Cal.App.4th 696 explains, sec. 2106 has been limited to those situations in which an award of damages would not hinder or frustrate the CPUC's declared regulatory policy. Sec. 1759 bars an action under sec. 2106 not only when an award of damages would directly contravene a specific decision of the CPUC, but also when an award would simply have the effect of undermining a general regulatory policy. The CPUC has determined that SmartMeters can be installed and pose no undue safety risk. Plaintiff's suit clearly attempts to undermine that policy. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Reporter: Sherry Sawyer #5976.

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-10-2014 Sustained
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Donald Zimmer, Esq., 415.318.1220, for defendant Landis + Gyr, Inc.; No other appearances.

RULING: The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT LANDIS+GYR, INC'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT is sustained without leave to amend because the Superior Court lacks jurisdiction to entertain the instant suit pursuant to Cal. Public Utilities Code sec. 1759, San Diego Gas & Electric Co. v. Sup. Crt. (1996) 13 Cal.4th 893 ("Covalt") and Ford et al v. Pacific Gas and Electric Co. (1997) 60 Cal.App.4th 696. See CCP 430.10(a). In San Diego Gas & Electric Co. v. Sup. Crt. (1996) 13 Cal.4th 893, 917-918 ("Covalt"), the court provides that the CPUC has exclusive jurisdiction where "(1) the commission had the authority to adopt a regulatory policy; (2) the commission has exercised its authority; and (3) the superior action would hinder or interfere with the commission's exercise of regulatory authority." Id. 13 Cal.4th 917-918. Here, the defendants correctly note that the CPUC has the authority to adopt SmartMeter policy under Public Utilities Code secs. 768, 451, 761, and 762. The CPUC exercised its authority, allowed the installation of the Smart Meters, and concluded that they were safe and in compliance with FCC regulations. Allowing this action to proceed would interfere with the CPUC's regulatory authority. Plaintiff argues that the action is proper under Cal. Public Utilities sec. 2106. As Ford et al v. Pacific Gas and Electric Co. (1997) 60 Cal.App.4th 696 explains, sec. 2106 has been limited to those situations in which an award of damages would not hinder or frustrate the CPUC's declared regulatory policy. Sec. 1759 bars an action under sec. 2106 not only when an award of damages would directly contravene a specific decision of the CPUC, but also when an award would simply have the effect of undermining a general regulatory policy. The CPUC has determined that SmartMeters can be installed and pose no undue safety risk. Plaintiff's suit clearly attempts to undermine that policy. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Reporter: Sherry Sawyer #5976.
